segments.title: Song and Yuan Buddhist art at Kyoto National Museum
segments.summary: An exhibition of Song and Yuan Buddhist painting prompts a close, non-specialist response to expressive figures, textiles, and ceramics.
segments.body: At the Kyoto National Museum, Dr. Clarke finds the main exhibition closed but an exhibition of Song and Yuan Buddhist painting open. He explicitly approaches it as a visitor rather than an art historian, focusing on the visual force of the works instead of technical classification. The lines and faces make the figures seem to leap from the surface, while the textiles and ceramics impress him through the skill of their making. His reaction offers a useful watch point for how viewers can engage seriously with unfamiliar historical art without pretending to specialist expertise. The segment reports a personal museum encounter rather than a comprehensive account of Song or Yuan art.

evidence.note: He introduces the Song and Yuan Buddhist art exhibition and describes the expressive figures, textiles, and ceramics that impressed him.

segments.title: Reading lacquered wooden Buddhas through craftsmanship
segments.summary: Family experience of carpentry helps Dr. Clarke notice the chisel work, fine detail, shaping, and bending in lacquered wooden Buddha figures.
segments.body: The lower gallery's lacquered wooden Buddha figures give Dr. Clarke a more direct route into the exhibition because carpentry ran through his family. He looks at the chisel work, fine detail, bending, shaping, and forming of the wood rather than simply calling the objects beautiful. That vocabulary connects historical religious sculpture with hands-on knowledge of furniture making. The comparison does not identify a specific carving technique or date, but it makes material skill the learning payoff. This is a useful watch point for seeing how close observation of craft can make museum objects intelligible to a non-specialist.

evidence.note: He connects the lacquered wooden Buddha figures with family carpentry experience and names their chisel work, fine detail, bending, and shaping.
